title: Dog One Testicle Larger Than the Other: Causes and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticed your dog has uneven testicles? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why a photo can help your veterinarian with a diagnosis.
content_html:
What Is It?

When one testicle appears larger than the other in a dog, it is known as testicular asymmetry. This condition can be caused by several factors, including testicular tumors, inflammation (orchitis), infection (epididymitis), or even a scrotal hernia. While finding a lump or swelling can be stressful, many of these issues are manageable when caught early by a veterinarian.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is generally Low. If your dog is behaving normally, eating well, and does not seem to be in pain, you can typically wait a few days for a scheduled veterinary appointment. However, if the scrotum is extremely red, hot to the touch, or if your dog is lethargic and vomiting, you should contact a clinic sooner as these could be signs of a more acute infection or torsion.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area can be a valuable tool for veterinary triage. A visual reference allows a professional to assess the scale of the swelling, the condition of the skin, and the specific shape of the enlargement. This information helps the veterinary team determine how quickly your dog needs to be seen and helps them prepare for the necessary diagnostic tests before you even arrive at the clinic.

faqs.question: Could it be cancer?
faqs.answer: Testicular tumors are a common cause of enlargement in intact older dogs. While many are benign, a veterinarian will likely recommend neutering to remove the affected tissue and prevent further issues.

faqs.question: Is it painful for my dog?
faqs.answer: In many cases of gradual enlargement, dogs do not show obvious signs of pain. However, if the swelling is caused by an infection or injury, the area may be very sensitive.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery?
faqs.answer: The most common treatment for testicular enlargement is orchiectomy, or neutering. This curative procedure removes both testicles and is generally the recommended course of action for tumors or chronic infections.
